If we do proceed with upgrading `@pulumi/pulumi`'s TypeScript, it would be worth checking if it impacts the `@pulumi/pulumi` docs generation, by running [`./scripts/run_typedoc.sh`](https://github.com/pulumi/docs/blob/master/scripts/run_typedoc.sh) in the docs repo. It's possible we may need to upgrade `typedoc` in that repo.

The other thing to be aware of is that this means any Pulumi programs written in TypeScript and using the built-in `ts-node` will now be using this new version of TypeScript. Which might require users to modify their Pulumi program (similar to the modifications we had to make in this PR), depending on what language features are being used. If there are things in their program that need to change, aside from changing their code, a workaround would be to use whatever version of TypeScript they want to compile their Pulumi program to JavaScript, and let Pulumi run the compiled JavaScript. If we want to avoid any of these potential issues for users, we could hold off on doing the upgrade until our next major release. (There is an alternative we can consider described below).

> This PR kinda goes against the premise of #7184 since updating typescript actually makes this dependency size larger.

From Slack, the change is 50MB->60MB.

On the one hand, I personally wouldn't hold up upgrading TypeScript over 10MB. It seems like even more of a reason to consider addressing #7184.

On the other hand, there is an alternative we could consider. Right now, our generated provider SDKs are compiled using the TypeScript dependency that is pulled in implicitly from the dependency on `@pulumi/pulumi`. Instead of upgrading `@pulumi/pulumi`'s dependency, we could consider changing our generated provider SDKs to have a devDependency on the latest TypeScript — and use that version of TypeScript to compile the provider SDKs.

i noticed that with these new changes, we've lost the ability to redirect to our original intended page after selecting a facility, and that's functionality that is important for us to retain. (e.g. working off your branch, when a multi-facility user clicks on "settings" and then selects a facility, they're no longer redirected to that facility's "settings" page the way a user would be on the current `develop` branch)


https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/assets/43046460/29e4dd08-f138-4ece-81b2-db07dbff3e55



the redirect as it is written now is looking to `params` to know which page to redirect to. because you removed `params` on what was originally line 31, it's no longer functioning as intended.

i think the most straightforward solutions here are either to add `params: { subtopicName },` back into this file in the place it had been _or_ change the way we take in the redirect information to now look to `query` params instead of `params`.

I've reviewed this with the premise that is a module migration to work with RDM but is not the intention to leave the code 100% clean and fix previous problems (although many were done anyway). I tried to review as much as possible, but is very hard to make an in-depth review in a sensible time due to its size; so my comments are more on structure than actual code implementation.

Tiny rant, nothing personal:

I'm seeing this (very big PRs, I've done it too), and I think we are setting ourselves for failure. Big PRs stay very long in the review column since they are very intimidating. For future reference, it would be nice to have more self contained PRs.
- Black and other migrations
- Example app removal
- Actual code/functionality changes

Having all in one PR with 54 files and 2.2/2.7k changes is quite unmanagable and very time consuming to review. Half of the brain power goes to understand if the change was black formatting or actual code. We can review commits, but in this case there's 5 on the actual code so there's still a need to jump from commit to commit.

Honestly, even GitHub lags to handle a PR of this size 😂 

Comments apply in many places related to layers:

- We are importing `NoResultsFound` in some classes and in some we are not.
- There is an `api.py` module and others get an actual name, e.g. `receivers.py`, nonetheless many seem to be APIs. 
- A clear separation between whats a model and what's an API, and even maybe splitting some of those API classes.
- I'd suggest to have an `/api` folder and then tiny classes inside since it seems this module does not need an actual service layer but it can be okay with just an api to bridge services (in rdm-records) to the GH api.

> I don't know if this is supposed to be acceptable, but I thought it may at least be worth mentioning.

Maybe someday we could "translate" that constraint to "not null" in `tables.sql`, but for the moment I think it's fine.  I am surprised it's not showing up in `schema.md`, though.  #4063.

I agree that dropping the functions and using `if not exists` is awkward.  Failing to do that might leave "stale" migration functions around that would be run unexpectedly.  I suspect that the JS that's running these migrations should just remove the expected functions (if they exist) before calling the migrationScript and downgradeScript - then there's no need to worry about dropping them at all, nor of executing "stale" functions.  I'm happy to add that in a followup if you'd like - just file an issue.

This patch looks fantastic.  I agree that the tests are sufficiently thorough.  Maybe a good double-check would be to introduce bugs in the various redefined DB functions and check that the tests fail?

Good spot that as of the completion of version 0055's migrationScript, the table invariant is that provisioner_id/worker_type OR task_queue_id are set, or maybe both.  That means the redefined functions in 0055 must account for either of those possibilities -- as you've done.  That's definitely a subtle point, but I'm not sure how to make it less subtle..

One thing that's missing is to use the `state` to start the migration batch at the next taskId that needs attention.  As written, toward the end of the process the batch will spend most of its time scanning rows where the columns are already not null.

The other thing to change is to define the online downgrade functions in the `downgradeScript` parameter in version 0056, rather than `migrationScript`.  With that change, I think it makes a little more sense to have 0056's online downgrade be the "mirror image" of 0055's online migration, as it's more of a "followup to 0056's downgradeScript" mirroring the "followup to 0055's migrationScript".

Leaving the function changes to a second (third!) PR sounds fine.

Thanks for the PR @timkpaine, this is a monstrous effort that is much appreciated.  I'm excited for the zero-config `perspective-python`/Jupyter integration future.

A couple of things stick out that I'd like you to elaborate on -

* I understand the reasoning behind renaming `@finos/perspective-jupyterlab` -> `@finos/perspective-jupyter`, but not the impact it will have on existing Jupyter users with the former already installed.  IMO this is _really important_, as the nature of this PR is to simplify installation, we don't want to co-introduce _arbitrary_ regressions.  If you think this is really important to stay, we should add correct upper bounds to the old package and publish it (rather than delete this package),  so that current users of the old package do not suddenly get deprecation errors on update, but rather a clear version-bound error saying that `@finos/perspective-jupyterlab` is incompat with the `perspective-python` bounds.  I believe (?) we can set the NPM deprecation message on that version to point to the new package.
* Please, please, for my own sanity - do not introduce `webpack` back into our build process, especially not to generate a full inline build _twice_.  I _finally_ managed to remove `webpack` entirely in favor of `esbuild`.  Even for a less-frequently-updated package like this, it is disruptive in the extreme, and Jupyter itself already imposes its own build tax on our precious development time.  I need to dig in a bit more (if you want me to), but I see no reason this can't be built in `esbuild` with the existing plugins in worst case;  but what would likely be even easier is to just import the pre-built inline builds of `@finos/perspective` and `@finos/perspective-viewer` directly, then `esbuild` these. looks great overall! just a couple of questions/nits

one broader questions i want to clarify is what assumptions we are making when accessing the payload of the request object.

if the request is a form request and contains a form-encoded body, the following calls will return
```python
request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads from the stream, caches the body, and returns it
request.form                   # parses the form data from the cache and returns the form data
request.get_data(as_text=True) # returns the cached body (will contain the original form-encoded body!)
````

whereas calling:
```python
request.data                   # reads from the stream, parses the form data, caches the form data and caches the remaining body (which is now empty, and will return `""`)
request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads and returns the cached body (which is empty)
request.form                   # returns the cached form data
request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads and returns the cached body (which is empty)
```

see here for a detailed explanation: https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/blob/bb21bf90b0b121e3ed45b9950b823e4b43a81fd8/src/werkzeug/wrappers/request.py#L385-L390

so that the parser will potentially behave differently depending on previous calls to `request.data` or `request.get_data(parse_form_data=True)`.
i'm not sure exactly how to address this.

after writing all this and thinking a bit more about the werkzeug API, i think it may be the best call to NOT call `get_data` at all, and just rely on the properties `request.data`, `request.values`, `request.form`, `request.args`, etc, which seems to be the way it was intended.
you lose the bit of convenience that `as_text=True` provides, but at then the behavior will be consistent.

[Transferred from other PR] A few comments upon a quick skim.

At a higher level and more future looking, I think that we might benefit from changing the block future abstraction from `ObjectRef[Block]` with a `BlockMetadata` sidecar object to `Block[Union[Data, ObjectRef[Data]], Metadata]`, where the block wraps a data (future) object and block metadata that's known at `Block` instantiation time, and exposes pretty much the same APIs. I think that something like that would simplify a lot of the remaining implementation, where we're currently carting around both block (future) objects and their corresponding metadata, and we're trying to surface metadata `Block` APIs for an `ObjectRef[Block]` via a separate `BlockMetadata`, which is clunky.

[Eric]
> Yes, I considered this, but it is less explicit. The current implementation is extremely clear about what is going on, which is why I chose it.

We can talk about this more later, but we might want to address this before we invest a lot into the current model. IMO the block metadata conceptually belongs to the block, even when just looking at the Block API, which exposes a bunch of metadata accessors. Splitting the block metadata out from the block abstraction results in a lot of duplicated logic: whenever we're getting or manipulating a block or list of blocks, we have to do the same to the sidecar metadata or list of metadatas, and we're always having to zip lists of blocks and their corresponding metadata together, or return a tuple containing blocks and their corresponding metadata. That's all a colocation code smell that suggests that those two abstractions should be consolidated. I actually think that this would make the implementation more clear, and I think that it would eliminate a lot of duplicated logic and bookkeeping, and will make future development simpler.

I can take a look at a time-boxed refactor later this week if that sounds good to you.

I can only give a general comment that these are like reference documentation, rather than tutorials. The reference docstrings for all the high-level functions focus on one function, what it does, and what its options and defaults are. That's what these new pages do for the reducers, but they should be broader (and less detailed) than that. A tutorial should instead focus on a problem (e.g. preparing data for plots), naming the functions that can be brought to bear on that problem, with examples, but without providing anything like a complete description of their options.

The [ak.sum](https://awkward-array.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_auto/ak.sum.html) reference documentation is already more tutorial-like in that it explains the general consequences of reducing with variable-length and missing data. Arguably, that should be taken out of the reference and put into this tutorial on reducers. The same is true of the [ak.behavior](https://awkward-array.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ak.behavior.html) reference, which is very tutorial-like, but it can't be moved because other websites already point to it (Coffea GitHub, for one).

The tutorials need to be from the data analysts' perspective. That's why there should be some extra discussion of `ak.any` and `ak.all`, because even though they're reducers, they're in a different mental category than the other reducers because they operate on booleans. When a data analyst is working through logic like, "I need any jet-muon pair to have ΔR < 0.2," that's mentally different from thinking, "I need to sum over photon energies," so we'd want some extra text to draw attention to the fact that formally, they're the same type of function.

The default `axis=None`, but that was chosen to agree with NumPy's functions of the same names. In most circumstances, users are going to want to set `axis=-1`, there should be a section drawing attention to that.

Also, there should be some explanation of what `axis=0` means in the context of arrays of lists of particles. The left-alignment of variable-length lists illustrated by your diagram implies that the most stably identifiable objects should be first in each nested list, and the tail of random junk should be last. If we had chosen to generalize `axis != -1` by right-alignment, the opposite would be true. The consequences of this left-alignment choice needs to be brought to the attention of data analysis users.

The argmin and argmax documentation is in a separate page from the other reducers because they're used in a special way: the output of argmin/argmax is almost always used as a slice, to select the "best" something-or-other from each list in an array of lists. That is, "find the particle in each event with the highest pT" or even "find the particle's eta in each event with the highest particle-pT." These common tasks require more than one step: computing the array of values to min/maximize, getting the array of integers from argmin/argmax, and then applying it as a slice. To do this, it is necessary to pass `keepdims=True`, which is not default (because of NumPy), in order to have the array of integers be at the right depth. Empty lists come out as `None`, though with `keepdims=True`, you might want to `ak.fill_none` them with empty lists. There's a lot to think about in this, and unlike [ak.sum](https://awkward-array.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_auto/ak.sum.html), the [ak.argmin](https://awkward-array.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_auto/ak.argmin.html) and [ak.argmax](https://awkward-array.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_auto/ak.argmax.html) reference documentation doesn't cover it.

I should also point out that the default reducer behavior for empty lists depends on the reducer: [ak.sum](https://awkward-array.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_auto/ak.sum.html) of empty lists results in `0`, [ak.prod](https://awkward-array.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_auto/ak.prod.html) of empty lists results in `1`, etc. It's only `None` for min/max/argmin/argmax because min/max are operations without an identity in number systems without infinity ("semigroups"), whereas the others, including `ak.any`/`ak.all`, have identities ("monoids").

I think this is the source of our disconnect about whether we can finish all of these tutorials before PyHEP. It generally takes me a day to write one page, to include all of these things I'm thinking that users might need to know. Writing a reference docstring is quite a bit faster, since that's like filling out a form: you have to say what the arguments mean, what the return value will be, and if there are any special cases—not how it will be used in the context of a data analysis.

Do you want to have another try at this? It's okay if you don't—the list of things that I'm looking for (above) are in my head and a "guess what I'm thinking" game isn't an efficient way to go about it. On the other hand, if you think you have an idea of what these are supposed to be like now and just weren't oriented before, go ahead!

I think we could be more thorough about unit testing failure scenarios. Here are a couple scenarios where I'm not sure if it will work anymore. It'd be good to check that there are no regressions here:

- Object spill fails, no pull locations --> should retry object restore at the next tick.
- Object spill fails, all locations attempted --> should retry object restore at the next tick.
- OnLocationChange about a spilled URL and all pull locations empty while there are in-flight pulls --> should try object restore immediately

I'm a little worried about the robustness of the retry code overall. I'm not positive these cases are currently broken, but either way it's not great that there are so many possible corner cases. I got a little lost trying to figure out when the timer gets reset vs. not and what it means for the overall behavior if the timer is not reset.

I think it would be best to cut this PR down so it only guarantees "at most one pull request every retry interval" and ignore "don't retry a client until you've tried all the other clients" for now. Sorry, I know the majority of the PR is for the latter, but I'm not sure it's worth the complexity at the moment :/

So let me put some context for @vfdev-5 and for the people who read the thread to understand better @NicolasHug's concerns at https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/6209/files#r908517255.

First of all, we all probably agree that the way `functional_*` are structured are unfortunate. The APIs have been communicated to be private but then after v0.8 there is no clear indication. So to Nicolas' point, how are users supposed to know? There is definitely room for improvement on the deprecation policies we used previously and that's exactly what Nicolas is trying to do now. He is going through all the cases where this is not communicated properly, updates the docs and adds mechanisms in place to let users know.

Concerning the changes on this PR, I don't think the modifications are strictly necessary for the Transforms v2 API (perhaps they can be avoided?). Nevertheless I see a potential issue if we block this change. Either we agree or disagree with policies introduced on old version of TorchVision, we must respect them. The specific API was marked private using the deprecation process followed at that time (around version v0.8). I don't think we should go back and undo this now (aka consider it public using today's definition). This puts a very bad precedence for other parts of the API such as the C++ side of things where everything is considered private or the Beta APIs where there are no BC guarantees. So my concern here is that blocking this PR will open the gates for blocking other important work on private or Beta APIs on the future. My worry is that the next PR might require a modification on this private API and it will be blocked because are now reconsidering it to be public.

For the above reasons and because the PR is technically correct and on a private API, I will approve it. I will leave it to @vfdev-5 to decide if he wants to merge it or close because it's not critical to his work. Again, I agree with @NicolasHug's concerns here; I'm just equally concerned that if we adopt this very strict policy it's going to come back and bite us on the near future as it will slow down critical planned work. I think more discussions are necessary around these corner-cases once we are all back at the office. The good thing is, we just released TorchVision yesterday which means we got 3+ months until the next one so we can always revert the decision afterwards.

I can only review the Rust code (rather than the big picture of how it fits into Pants). I've also not looked through all the 'resolved' comments, so I might've been repeating some things.

My general comment is: nice!

Broadly speaking though, it feels like there's quite a lot of `unwrap`ing. I've commented on some specific ones, but there's also a lot of `name.named_child(...).unwrap()` or equivalent. I'd be nervous about this making dep inference fragile to invalid code, or syntax changes, or other unexpected problems, and bringing down the whole pantsd when they fail.

Some alternatives, depending on the situation:

- propagate errors with `?` etc.
- `.expect("<short explanation>")` or `unwrap_or_else(|| panic!("foo {some_value} bar"))` to at least give more context
- ignore silently or with some logging (e.g. if the code is invalid, just skip over)
If it has reached end-of-life I don't think we should stop testing with it just because we can, but it shouldn't be a barrier for development.

Thanks for the update @adetsi.  This implementation is better but the failing tests indicate another issue, that `median` can't be calculated for `integer` columns without changing the aggregate column's output type to `float`; trying to calculate the median without this will generate all 0's.  In order to do this, you'd need to set `t_tscalar median_average` explicitly, as well as setting the associated metadata for the schema transition in a few other places around the codebase. (this is done already for e.g. `count` which goes from any type to `integer`, if you need an example to grep for).

In lieu of this, it would be easier to just implement this behavior for `float` columns instead of _numeric_ ones, by changing the feature-check from `is_numeric` to `is_floating_point`.  `integer` columns will then stay `integer` when applied `median()` and exhibit the old behavior, while columns which are float will use the new behavior. This would be an acceptable alternative if the former implementation for `integer`-column-type-upgrading is too complex (it may involve updating python and the UI as well).

See inline review notes below, there are also a few small style and efficiency improvements, and we should preserve the current test inputs and their integer columns.

EDIT

I've [implement these changes here](https://github.com/finos/perspective/commit/51c6005c214554257a9bc3c6572fd2fcfbfee750).

IIUC, we are only using `.as_subclass(torch.Tensor)` here to avoid `__torch_function__` here, correct? If yes, why not simply use the context manager that disables this?

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/07ae61bf9c21ddd1d5f65d326aa9636849b383ca/torchvision/prototype/features/_feature.py#L102-L103

like

```py
with DisableTorchFunction():
    return self.shape
```

Better yet, instead of adding properties for all these attributes manually, why not overwrite `__getattribute__` to do this for us?

```py
    _NO_TORCH_FUNCTION = {
        "shape",
    }

    def __getattribute__(self, item):
        with DisableTorchFunction() if item in type(self)._NO_TORCH_FUNCTION else contextlib.nullcontext():
            return object.__getattribute__(self, item)
```

Test:

```py
import unittest.mock

import torch
from torchvision.prototype import features

image = features.Image(torch.rand(3, 16, 16))

with unittest.mock.patch(
    "torchvision.prototype.features._Feature.__torch_function__",
    side_effect=AssertionError,
):
    image.shape
```

I have not benchmarked this. If it is as fast or faster than your suggestion, I would prefer this for several reasons:

1. We don't need to add properties manual, which is harder to maintain.
2. Subclasses can extend `_NO_TORCH_FUNCTION` or whatever we call it to also avoid `__torch_function__` calls on the metadata they are adding, i.e. `Image.color_space`.
3. Since we don't need to unwrap anymore, we can also avoid the issues @datumbox mentioned in https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/6681/files#r986001975.

> Agree that this is weird, but I think that this weirdness is just inherited from the weirdness of @logger. In a world where there were only handlers, the two responsibilities would be very distinct: DagsterLogManager is a log that produces messages with Dagster metadata and has a set of handlers on it. DagsterLogConversionHandler is a special handler that sneakily converts normal python logs into Dagster-formatted logs, and then calls all of DagsterLogManager's handlers with this new record.

Just spitballin' here: what if we relied on the Python logging framework for dispatching instead of implementing on our own in `DagsterLogManager`.  E.g.  `context.log` would point to an object produced by invoking `logging.getLogger("__dagster__")`.  Then, for each Dagster `LoggerDefinition`, we'd create a logging `Handler` that wraps the `Logger` returned by the `LoggerDefinition`, and have that `Handler` subscribe to the "__dagster__" logger?
